This is in there because it's a weird occupation. And it's an interesting occupation, so there's a testimonial from magicians. This one is in there not so much because I was prospecting for any work from nonprofits, because I never did, not because I wanted work from nonprofits, but it got me a football player, and then it allowed me to get pictures in of the football player and the football player with a celebrity. Well, that's the with strategy, okay? I'm not with Ditka, but this guy's a client of mine and he's with Ditka, so that's the same as being with Ditka. Hartooneans is in there because it's a really, really, really good testimonial letter. You cannot overuse these things. It's impossible. You got to get them all the time, get as many as you can, keep getting them updated as your relationships with people continue over a period of time. Use them, use them, use them, use them, use them everywhere, because what they say about you is infinitely more persuasive than what you say about you. Last thing before we break. Let's have a quick discussion of ethics. Here's what I could, here's my advice to you about all of this. Number one, don't take clients you shouldn't. So you ought to define for yourself what that means. But the best advice I can give you is figure out what that means for you and then do everything you possibly can to resist the temptation to take one you know you shouldn't. I don't care how broke you are. If you take one you shouldn't, all that does for you is make you broker. You may temporarily keep a car from being repossessed, but trust me, it's not worth it. The car is replaceable. Your time, peace of mind, sanity of dealing with a client you shouldn't have taken, you can never get back. I have one from 25 years ago, I still occasionally wake up in the middle of the night thinking about her. And not in a good way. The money has long since been spent, the problem the money solved has long since been gone, somehow it would have been solved without her, the memory haunts me for life. The best reason not to take a client is you know you won't be able to satisfy him. Big mistake to take him if you know you won't be able to satisfy him. It doesn't make him bad. So the two examples I got here, they both for somebody would be and were great clients. And I like them personally, and I have enormous respect for them. Bill Phillips when he was at EAS, I would love to have worked with Bill Phillips, but I would never have been able to satisfy Bill Phillips. Because if Bill wakes up at 2 o'clock in the morning with an idea, he wants everybody on a conference call at 2.30, all the copy written, and there better be a half a million pieces in full color dropping in the mail by noon the next day. Now that don't make Bill a bad guy, that don't even make him wrong, but it makes him really wrong for me. Ken Roberts people, they are not going to be happy unless they can pick up the phone and talk to their copywriter. There is no way to make them happy unless they can do that. It doesn't matter, I could make them $50 million, they wouldn't be happy. And I, not even for them, and not even for all the money either one of them might have paid me, am I going to be picking up the phone and talking to people at random?
